title: router_misconfiguration
description: A misconfigured router causes routing loops and network instability after a change window.

timeline.title: Change Window Closes
timeline.description: A planned router configuration change is applied and the change window closes.

timeline.title: Routing Instability Detected
timeline.description: BGP neighbors begin flapping and CPU utilization on the router spikes to 100%.

timeline.title: Network Degradation Spreads
timeline.description: Routing loops cause packet loss across multiple network segments. User-facing services degrade.

timeline.title: Change Identified as Root Cause
timeline.description: Network team correlates timing and identifies the recent change introduced an incorrect static route.

timeline.title: Configuration Rollback Initiated
timeline.description: Engineers begin rolling back the router configuration to the pre-change baseline.

timeline.title: Network Stability Restored
timeline.description: Rollback completes. BGP sessions re-establish and routing tables converge normally.

node-1.title: Post-Change Network Instability
node-1.situation: Minutes after a planned router configuration change, network monitoring shows BGP flapping, routing loops, and 100% CPU on the core router. Multiple services are degraded. The change was applied by a junior engineer and the pre-change configuration was saved.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Declare an incident, halt any further changes, review the change log to identify what was modified, and assess whether an immediate rollback is warranted.

options.label: B
options.text: Apply additional configuration changes to try to fix the routing loop without reviewing what the previous change did.

options.label: C
options.text: Reboot the router immediately to clear the routing table and BGP sessions.

node-2.title: Root Cause Analysis
node-2.situation: The change log shows a new static route was added that points a /16 network back through the router itself, creating a routing loop. BGP is consuming all CPU trying to propagate the looping routes. The pre-change config backup is confirmed clean.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Immediately remove the incorrect static route to break the routing loop, then monitor BGP convergence before applying a full rollback to the clean baseline.

options.label: B
options.text: Perform a full router reboot to clear all state, which will also drop all BGP sessions for several minutes.

node-3.title: BGP Recovery
node-3.situation: The incorrect static route has been removed. Router CPU has dropped to 35%. BGP sessions are re-establishing but two peers have not yet converged. You need to decide whether to wait or intervene.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Monitor BGP session recovery passively for 5 minutes, then use soft-reset commands on unconverged peers if they do not re-establish naturally.

options.label: B
options.text: Immediately hard-reset all BGP sessions to force rapid reconvergence, causing a temporary complete routing outage.

node-4.title: Full Rollback and Validation
node-4.situation: All BGP sessions have converged. The network is stable. You now need to apply the full rollback to the clean baseline config and establish safeguards to prevent this recurrence.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Apply the full baseline rollback during a brief maintenance window, validate routing tables, implement a peer-review requirement for all router changes, and document the incident.

options.label: B
options.text: Leave the current partial configuration in place since BGP is stable and avoid the rollback to reduce risk.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You effectively identified the misconfigured static route causing the routing loop, surgically removed it to restore stability, allowed BGP to converge naturally, and implemented a peer-review change control improvement.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
